# Questions in Livestream 81

Live Stream #81: Happy Thanksgiving Weekend

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=645" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:45</a> | Is it impolite for people in chat to answer super chats, and what are the biggest problems with Exodus mythicism? | He welcomed informed chat participation and said major problems with Exodus mythicism include weak engagement with the evidence and overreliance on bad arguments from silence. | He said chat answers are welcome because the channel aims to teach viewers enough to answer objections themselves, though newcomers should first learn the channel's content and everyone should keep answers concise rather than proselytize. On Exodus mythicism, he criticized an ever-rising standard of evidence, argued that mythicism is unnecessary because one can hold a naturalistic Exodus without denying the historical core, and said mythicist explanations often fail to engage evidence such as Egyptian loanwords plausibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=1142" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:02</a> | Who likely chained the Gerasene demoniac possessed by `Legion`? | He said we cannot know for sure, but the family and some mix of local guards or strong men are more plausible than a neat Roman-soldier answer. | He framed the answer as speculative because the Gospels do not identify the people involved. His best guess was that the family would have been involved or given permission, and that local guards, strong men, or private security retainers could have helped; Roman soldiers were not the only armed or strong people available in the region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=1301" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:41</a> | If Jacob spent 20 years in Haran working for Laban, is the total sojourning in Canaan and Egypt 410 years rather than 430? | He had not worked out the full patriarchal chronology, but thought the 20 years in Haran were probably already accounted for in the 430-year figure. | He cautioned that he had not fully worked out the patriarchal chronology. Tentatively, he thought the 20 years in Haran were already included in the 430-year figure, while noting a Second Temple interpretation reflected in Acts 13 that may account for a roughly 450-year total by adding those 20 years; he repeatedly left room for his math or understanding to be wrong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=1562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:02</a> | What could have caused Josephus's report of a bright light at the temple around A.D. 66? | He did not know; he distinguished it from reports of heavenly signs that may have involved a locust swarm and said the temple light itself remained uncertain. | He said he had no clue what caused the temple light and had not studied Josephus or Tacitus on this point in depth. He distinguished it from other strange signs around the temple's destruction, such as possible locust-cloud imagery connected with chariots of heaven motifs, but the half-hour bright light itself remained unexplained for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=1806" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:06</a> | Is the `land of Sinim` in Isaiah 49:12 China or the Far East? | He said probably not; he favored a southern identification tied to Aswan / Syene instead. | He rejected China or the Far East as unlikely and instead identified the name with the far south, especially the Egyptian place-name for Aswan, across from Elephantine. He noted Aswan was already an important city by Isaiah's period and that later Jewish refugees reached Elephantine through Aswan, making a southern Egyptian identification more plausible in his reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=2031" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:51</a> | Did people in the ancient Near East, especially the late Bronze Age, practice polemical theology by trashing other nations' gods? | He said they polemicized against peoples, kings, and armies, but generally avoided trashing other gods; that kind of theological polemic is more characteristic of Israelite monotheism. | He said ancient Near Eastern powers used plenty of national and military polemic, such as calling enemies weak or wretched, but generally did not insult other gods directly. Because polytheistic cultures often treated foreign gods as real powers, they might claim those gods fled or abandoned a defeated people, or capture their images, but Israelite-style theological mockery of other gods was unusual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=2391" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:51</a> | What is your favorite popular-level book on Old Testament reliability besides Kitchen's book? | He recommended `Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament` as a lower-level academic resource, while noting he does not read many popular-press books. | He said he does not read much popular-press material, so his recommendation was not strictly a popular book. He suggested `Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament: Cultural, Social, and Historical Contexts` because it is more accessible, roughly early-college level, and many of its essays and contributors support a broadly maximalist view of Old Testament reliability even though the book is not solely about reliability. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=2577" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:57</a> | Are there good books on the resurrection, and do you believe Mary remained a virgin after Jesus's birth? | He did not have a strong resurrection bibliography ready, though chat suggested Andrew Loke, and he said he does not accept Mary's perpetual virginity. | He said he had studied Mary's perpetual virginity more than resurrection bibliography and did not know a strong resurrection book list offhand, though he looked toward Gordon-Conwell bibliography resources and accepted Andrew Loke as a likely good starting point when chat suggested him. On Mary, he rejected perpetual virginity, saying marriage involved conjugal relations, Mary served a special purpose but was still a sinner saved by grace, and he had not seen a compelling argument that she remained a virgin after Jesus's birth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=3471" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:51</a> | What should we make of Jephthah's vow in Judges 11 to offer whoever came out of his house as a burnt offering? | He read it as an actual burnt offering and human sacrifice, emphasizing that Jephthah was a bad judge and that God had not demanded the vow. | He argued that Jephthah's wording was dangerously vague because Israelite four-room houses could contain animals below and family above, so the vow allowed either an animal or a person to meet him. Because the text says burnt offering, he rejected attempts to soften it into lifelong virginity and read it as human sacrifice. The point, in his view, is that Jephthah was foolish, rash, bargaining with God, and part of Judges' downward spiral rather than a hero to be defended. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=4155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:15</a> | Why does Job suffer? | He said the immediate reason remains hazy, but the book lets readers reflect on suffering, faithfulness, and whether they would remain faithful if everything were taken away. | He said the small-picture reason for Job's suffering is not fully explained, even though the prologue shows a divine-council drama in which the accuser tests Job's faithfulness. In the larger picture, he said Job is not mainly a comfort book for someone already suffering but a reflective book for those whose lives are stable, asking whether they would remain faithful if wealth, family, and health were all taken away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=5078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:38</a> | Is Hinduism like a successful version of Atenism? | He said they are comparable as pantheistic systems, but Hinduism is polytheistic while Atenism was monolatrous; he tied their different outcomes partly to priestly and economic support structures. | He said the comparison is useful because both systems moved from polytheistic roots toward pantheistic concepts of divinity. The difference was that Hinduism remained a polytheistic pantheism, while Atenism was monolatrous and dependent on royal patronage. Hinduism endured, in his view, because its priestly and social structures kept ritual support flowing, whereas Egyptian cults needed money and royal backing, and Atenism died when that support stopped. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=5486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:26</a> | Besides Kenneth Kitchen, James Hoffmeier, and you, are there other Egyptologists who defend the Exodus? | He said yes, though many Egyptologists simply do not care about the question; he mentioned Muslim Egyptologists, Mark Janzen, Manfred Bietak, and Charles Aling with caveats. | He said some Egyptologists do believe in or defend an Exodus, while many simply regard the question as irrelevant to Egyptology rather than actively deny it. He noted that many Egyptologists are Muslim and accept an Exodus because Islam does, mentioned Mark Janzen and Manfred Bietak with qualifications about Bietak's changing chronology, and cited Charles Aling as an early-date defender while also qualifying his evaluation of Aling's Egyptological strength. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=6415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:55</a> | Does `Ur` in Genesis 11:31 mean `fire of the Chaldeans`? | He said no, not fire; he thought the claim likely depends on a bad handling of Hebrew pointing or spacing, while noting the verse may still have a text-critical issue. | He said the phrase is definitely not "fire of the Chaldeans" and suggested the claim probably mishandles Hebrew pointing or treats the word as light before turning it into fire. He added that Genesis 11:31 may still have a real text-critical issue because early Hebrew lacked spaces, so the Masoretic spacing could be debated; he was interested in a possible alternative involving Erkesh, but not the "fire" reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=6913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:13</a> | Would Moses, as the adopted son of Pharaoh's daughter in `Exodus: Gods and Kings`, have had a stronger claim to the throne than Ramses? | No. He said adoption by a king's daughter would not give Moses a throne claim, though it could place him within the royal family and allow access to the king. | He said adoption by Pharaoh's daughter would not make Moses a king's son or give him succession rights. Egyptian succession favored sons of the king, especially sons of the great queen, and a daughter could not independently create a throne claim by adopting someone. At most, royal adoption might place Moses within the royal household and give him access to the king, unless the king himself recognized him as a son. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=7284" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:24</a> | Where would the lodging place between Midian and Egypt in Exodus 4 have been? | He said the Hebrew term is very broad and could mean an inn, cave, oasis, campsite, or village-edge lodging; the text does not give enough detail to identify it. | He identified the Hebrew term as broad and vague, covering possibilities such as an inn, cave, oasis, campsite, rented structure near an oasis, or even lodging on the outskirts of a village. Because Moses and Zipporah were moving in a Midianite context where pitching camp could be normal, and because the passage lacks narrowing details, he said the location cannot be identified more precisely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=7817" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:17</a> | Is there a resource for understanding biblical idioms? | He did not know a dedicated resource offhand and usually checks good commentaries, but said such a resource should exist because biblical idioms are important and often colorful. | He said he did not know a dedicated biblical-idiom resource offhand, though he expected one should exist and usually turns to good biblical commentaries. He illustrated why such a resource would be valuable with idioms like cutting a covenant, the idiomatic use of forty, and the Jacob-Laban phrase often romanticized in modern contexts even though he reads it as hostile or threatening in context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=8156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:56</a> | How should we understand the long ages of the patriarchs in Genesis 5? | He favored reading them as clan longevity and abbreviated clan genealogies rather than literal individual lifespans or merely symbolic ages. | He said views range from literal long individual lifespans to symbolic ages, but he prefers a clan-based reading. Since Israelite society was organized around clans, he sees Genesis 5 as preserving succession and longevity of clans rather than every individual generation. He also appealed to broad Hebrew father-son language and abbreviated genealogies, arguing that key clan founders can be listed while intermediate generations are omitted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=8649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:09</a> | Would Israel leaving Egypt with gold and silver in Exodus 12:35 have caused Egyptian economic hardship? | He said no, because Egypt used a barter economy pegged to goods and bronze weight, not a silver-and-gold currency economy. | He said Egypt did not have a money-based economy where gold and silver functioned as ordinary currency. Goods were bartered and valued against measures such as bronze deben, while gold and silver were largely elite, funerary, or decorative materials. Removing articles of precious metal therefore would not collapse the economy, especially since much of that metal would otherwise end up in graves or elite goods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=8917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:37</a> | How does the Quran differ from Egyptian history in its Exodus material? | He said the Quran treats Pharaoh as if it were a name, includes Haman, and says Pharaoh died in the sea, all of which he saw as historically problematic. | He said the Quran affirms an Exodus but gets important historical details wrong. In his examples, it treats Pharaoh as though it were a personal name rather than a title, includes Haman in a way he found historically problematic, and says Pharaoh died in the Sea of Reeds. He contrasted that with Egyptian mummy evidence, saying known royal mummies do not show the kinds of drowning indicators he would expect from such an event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=9181" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:01</a> | Are there Bronze Age Israelite or Egyptian parallels to the later Jewish idea of an angel of death? | He said Israel has death-dealing angel language, while Ugaritic `Mot` is a useful comparison; Egypt, by contrast, had many death-related gods but no simple god of death. | He said Israelite tradition does include divine or angelic death-dealing language, such as the Exodus context, but Bronze Age parallels are limited. Ugaritic religion provides the best comparison in Mot, a god of death involved in the Baal cycle. Egypt, despite its intense concern with death, had gods connected with resurrection, embalming, protection, and funerary processes, but he said it did not have a straightforward god of death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=9389" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:29</a> | Why did the debate with Titus Kennedy feel more like a debate than a discussion? | He said it was meant to be a conversation, but Kennedy opened with a Gish gallop, forcing him to answer point by point rather than set a calmer conversational agenda. | He said the event was supposed to be a conversation, but Kennedy's opening stacked multiple prooftexts in a way he identified as a Gish gallop. That forced him either to ignore the biblical claims and concede the perceived high ground, or answer each point and sound combative. He also thought the event structure did not allow each side to set a calm agenda before moving into the issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=10074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:54</a> | Could Isaiah 51:10's references to the deep and the redeemed be a prophetic reference to Christ's victory over death? | He said it works typologically through the Exodus and Passover, with Christ as the new Passover lamb, but it is not a direct Messianic prophecy. | He read Isaiah 51:10 as an Exodus and Sea of Reeds reference, with "redeemed" tied especially to those bought through the Passover blood. Because the New Testament presents Christ as the new Passover lamb, he said the passage can function typologically for Christ's victory, but he distinguished that from a direct Messianic prophecy like Psalm 2. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=10370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:50</a> | Do you think others will still debate you? | He thought some people may avoid debating him for a while, and said he dislikes debates, is selective, and generally wants qualified opponents and a serious format. | He said he expects some potential opponents to avoid debate for a while and emphasized that he does not enjoy debates or panels. He has become selective, generally avoiding opponents without a PhD because too much time goes into basic data rather than higher-level argument. He took the Titus Kennedy debate because Kennedy was one of the few active, high-profile early-date advocates with relevant credentials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=10904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:44</a> | Who was Amun-Re, did he have incarnations, and is he related to Jesus? | He said Amun-Re was a composite Egyptian deity of Amun and Re, had no actual existence or incarnation, and has no relation to Jesus. | He described Amun-Re as a composite Egyptian god joining Amun, the hidden god, with Re, the sun god, and associated him especially with Theban dynastic religion from the Middle Kingdom onward. He denied that Amun-Re had actual existence, denied that this deity incarnated in Egyptian religion, and said there is no relationship between Amun-Re and Jesus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=11106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:06</a> | Do you believe in a pre-wrath rapture? | No. He said many rapture prooftexts are better read as the summary punishment of unbelievers, and he rejected pre-wrath rapture exegesis. | He said he does not hold a hard view on every eschatological question, but he thinks the rapture is often misunderstood. In his reading, many passages used for a rapture of believers actually refer to the summary punishment of unbelievers at the day of the Lord, in imagery such as separation, judgment, the winepress, or the marriage supper. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=11306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:26</a> | In ancient Hebrew cosmology, was Sheol below, above, or the same as the deep? | He said the deep appears below the earth and Sheol below that, with Jonah 2 showing a descent through the deep toward Sheol. | He distinguished the deep from Sheol, saying the deep appears to be an under-earth body of water, with Sheol conceptually below it. He pointed to Jonah 2 as showing a downward movement through the deep toward Sheol. He added that this is analogical spiritual reasoning in ancient cosmology, not necessarily a literal map of the universe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=11446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:46</a> | Are Ethiopian Jewish claims best explained as Israelite colonies or as Judahite groups established after the exile? | He said neither exactly; he connected them with later diaspora Jews and a Jewish kingdom in Ethiopia, while warning that he was speaking outside his strongest period. | He said the explanation was neither early Israelite colonies nor simply Judahite groups returning after exile. Speaking cautiously outside his main period, he connected Ethiopian Jewish claims with later diaspora Jews in Arabia and a period when a Jewish ruler or kingdom in Ethiopia drew Jews into the region. He stressed they were still descendants of Israel, but through later diaspora movements. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=11913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:33</a> | How would Dr. Falk Studios develop a Bible-based game? | He said resources would shape the format, open-world games are too expensive, and he would prefer games that reflect a biblical worldview rather than directly caricature biblical stories. | He said the format would depend entirely on resources, since modern open-world games require enormous budgets. He had experimented with lower-cost game tools such as Ren'Py, but said his main priority would be avoiding Bible caricature. Rather than directly turning biblical stories into simplistic gameplay, he would prefer games with good story, fun play, and a worldview shaped by biblical ideals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=12185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:05</a> | Is the serpent in Eden Satan incarnate like an Egyptian god in animal form? | No. He said the serpent is a spirit being, later identified with Satan, appearing by manifestation rather than incarnation. | He said Genesis does not simply call the serpent an animal; he treated the Hebrew term as a pun that can suggest serpent imagery and a shining or bronze-like being. He connected the figure with a fallen cherub later identified as Satan, but not as an incarnation. In his view, before the fall Adam and Eve could encounter spirit beings directly, so this was a manifestation of a cherub rather than a god taking animal incarnation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=12532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:52</a> | How did Egyptians calculate a king's first regnal year, by accession or non-accession reckoning? | He said Egypt used accession-style reckoning, so even a very short first calendar year could count as the king's year, unlike Mesopotamian non-accession reckoning. | He contrasted Egyptian practice with Mesopotamian non-accession reckoning. In Egypt, the king's regnal count began when he came to the throne, so even one remaining day in the year could be counted as that king's first year. This difference, he said, helps explain why synchronizing Egyptian and Mesopotamian dates can produce one- or two-year offsets. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=12679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:19</a> | Is it possible that Moses and the prophets were high on psychedelic drugs and imagined they were talking to God? | He said ancient Egyptians had some psychoactive substances, but he saw little opportunity or evidence for Moses or Israelite prophets using them, and treated such claims as very weak. | He acknowledged that Egyptians had access to substances such as Cypriot opium and lotus preparations, but said Moses in Sinai would not have had that context and that evidence for Israelite or Midianite prophetic psychedelic use is very thin. He also warned that some residue claims can be contaminated by later activity at archaeological sites, so he would require much stronger evidence before accepting the claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Fg5vmXieQmg?t=13098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:18</a> | What are your thoughts on the deconversion of people with higher education, especially Ben Stanhope? | He said higher education is not the core cause; he emphasized rigid fundamentalist hermeneutics and early formation as more important factors, with education sometimes becoming a tool in deconversion. | He said Ben Stanhope's deconversion did not surprise him because he saw it as rooted in hermeneutics and formation rather than education by itself. In his view, people raised with rigid fundamentalist interpretive frameworks may keep that same rigidity even after leaving faith, using higher education as a tool in the process. He clarified that multiple possible interpretations do not mean the Bible is errant or arbitrary, but that interpretations themselves are not inspired. |
